1: When judges decide a case, "acquitting the innocent and condemning the guilty" — the ideal of the judicial system. Christ as the ultimate judge holds this standard perfectly: he is the "righteous judge" (2 Timothy 4:8) who "will judge the world with righteousness and the peoples with his truth" (Psalms 96:13). Unlike human judges who fail, he judges without partiality and without error (Acts 17:31: he has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness).
2: If the guilty party deserves flogging, the judge has him struck in his presence with the appropriate number of lashes. The judicial oversight of punishment — the judge is present and accountable. At Jesus's trial, Pilate had him flogged (John 19:1; Matthew 27:26) without the procedural requirements of Deuteronomy 25:2. The judicial irregularity of Christ's flogging is part of the unjust trial the NT consistently emphasizes — YHWH's righteous judgment (Acts 2:23: "delivered over according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God") operates through and despite the unjust human process.
3: "He may receive up to forty lashes, but no more." The limit — to preserve the dignity of the covenant member. Jewish practice counted forty "minus one" (39 lashes) to ensure they never accidentally exceeded the limit. Paul received "forty lashes minus one" five times from Jewish authorities (2 Corinthians 11:24) — the mark of the apostle who suffered within the covenant discipline. Christ received Roman flogging, which had no legal limit: his suffering exceeded every covenant protection, bearing the full weight of the curse that the law's limits were designed to contain.
4: "Do not muzzle an ox while it is threshing." The animal laboring to produce grain is entitled to eat of it. Paul cites this verse in 1 Corinthians 9:9–10: "was it for oxen that God was concerned? Does he not certainly speak for our sake? It was written for our sake." He then applies it to apostolic financial support: those who labor in the gospel are entitled to material support from those who benefit. Paul also cites it in 1 Timothy 5:18 alongside Jesus's saying "the laborer deserves his wages" — a Torah verse and a Jesus-word placed side by side as equally authoritative Scripture.
5: The levirate law — the brother-in-law must marry the widow and raise up a son in the dead brother's name. Two key NT connections: (1) The Sadducees use this law to pose their resurrection puzzle to Jesus (Matthew 22:23–33; Mark 12:18–27; Luke 20:27–40); (2) Boaz acts as kinsman-redeemer for Ruth (Ruth 3–4), the positive fulfillment of the levirate principle, which places him in the Messianic lineage (Matthew 1:5). Christ is the ultimate kinsman-redeemer who restores the inheritance of those who had lost it through death.
6: "The first son she bears shall carry on the name of the dead brother, so that his name is not wiped out from Israel." The concern for the perpetuation of the name — no man should be blotted out from the covenant community's memory. Christ guarantees that those who belong to him will not be blotted from the book of life (Revelation 3:5): "I will never blot out the name of the one who conquers from the book of life." The levirate law's concern for name-perpetuation finds its ultimate answer in Christ who ensures his people's names are permanently inscribed.
7: "My brother-in-law refuses to perpetuate his brother's name in Israel." The man's refusal to fulfill the levirate duty is a public failure of covenant responsibility. Boaz's willingness to redeem when the nearer kinsman refused (Ruth 3:12–Deuteronomy 4:10) is the positive type: the one who was willing stepped forward when the other could not or would not. Christ came when no other could redeem — when human intermediaries were inadequate to the task of kinsman-redemption for all humanity (Hebrews 7:23–25).
8: The elders summon the reluctant brother and give him opportunity to reconsider. The pastoral intervention before judgment — persuasion before sanction. Jesus's own practice follows this pattern: he calls individuals, debates, reasons, and invites before pronouncing judgment. The pattern of "I have spoken to you" (John 15:22: "if I had not come and spoken to them, they would not be guilty of sin") — opportunity precedes accountability.
9: The widow removes his sandal and spits in his face, declaring "this is what is done to the man who will not build up his brother's family." The sandal-removal ritual marks shame and public dishonor. John the Baptist's declaration — "I am not worthy to remove the sandals of the one who comes after me" (John 1:27; Mark 1:7) — may carry levirate resonance: Christ is the kinsman-redeemer who does what the inadequate forerunner cannot; John points away from himself precisely as the unworthy one who cannot perform the redemption.
10: "His family shall be known in Israel as 'The house of the unsandaled man.'" The public shame-naming. The covenant community's memory of failure and faithlessness. Peter's denial of Christ — three times before the crowing of the rooster (Matthew 26:69–75) — is a levirate-style failure: the kinsman-brother who would not stand with the one who needed him. But Christ's restoration of Peter (John 21:15–19) reverses the shame: the one who denied becomes the shepherd of the flock.
11: The law penalizing the woman who grabs an opponent's genitals while defending her husband — protecting the man's reproductive capacity. One of the law's most anatomically specific provisions. The underlying concern is the potential disruption of the lineage — Israel's future depends on the continuity of families. Christ is the one in whom the entire concern for biological lineage reaches its terminus: "in him all the promises of God are Yes" (2 Corinthians 1:20). The spiritual family he creates — "whoever does the will of my Father in heaven is my brother and sister and mother" (Matthew 12:50) — supersedes but does not erase the biological family the law protects.
12: "You shall cut off her hand. Show no pity." The severity of the penalty for this specific act points to how seriously the law protects the capacity for life and lineage. The principle of no-pity in judicial execution appears also in Deuteronomy 19:21 (eye for eye). Jesus's "show no pity" statements in the Sermon on the Mount are consistently followed by the alternative of inward transformation: the external legal sanction points beyond itself to the heart-level purity Christ requires and provides (Matthew 5:27–30).
13: "Do not carry two different sets of weights in your bag — one heavier, one lighter." Business integrity — not using different measures to your advantage in different transactions. The double standard is the essence of the dishonesty that characterizes the commercial exploitation of the poor. Christ is "the faithful and true witness" (Revelation 3:14) and the one in whom the single, honest standard holds: "with the measure you use, it will be measured to you" (Matthew 7:2). The honest-weights law becomes the law of the kingdom's judgment.
14: "Do not keep two different measures in your house — one larger, one smaller." The same principle repeated for domestic measures — the dishonesty of selectively expanding or contracting the measure. The law envisions a person who uses one measure when buying (large, to receive more) and another when selling (small, to give less). Christ's single standard applies equally in all directions: "do to others as you would have them do to you" (Matthew 7:12) — the same measure for receiving and giving.
15: "You shall have accurate and honest weights and measures, so that you may live long in the land the LORD your God is giving you." The life-promise attached to honest commerce. The new covenant equivalent: Paul's vision of a community where no one is cheated (1 Thessalonians 4:6) and where the truthfulness of speech is extended to all commercial and social dealings (Ephesians 4:25). Christ as the ground of truth in all human dealings — honest measures become a participation in his character as the faithful and true one.
16: "Everyone who does these things — all who act dishonestly — is an abomination to the LORD your God." The category of abomination applied to commercial dishonesty — not merely ritual uncleanness. Jesus's cleansing of the temple (Matthew 21:12–13; John 2:13–16) was an act against commercial exploitation within the covenant community's most sacred space: "you have made it a den of robbers." The money-changers' exploitation of worshipers was the abomination of dishonest weights in a sacred setting.
17: "Remember what the Amalekites did to you on the road when you came out of Egypt." Memory as the ground of both vigilance and eventual justice. The Amalekites' attack on Israel's vulnerable rear represents predatory opportunism against the weak — "they had no fear of God" (v18). Christ's eschatological judgment addresses all such predation: the judgment of the sheep and goats (Matthew 25:31–46) brings final accountability for every act against the vulnerable.
18: "They attacked you on the road and cut down all those who were straggling at the rear — the faint and the weary — when you were exhausted. They had no fear of God." The attack on the most vulnerable — those who were already exhausted and at the back of the column. Christ's parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:30–37) describes a similar roadside attack; the question of who will help those who fall behind is answered by the one who "has compassion," who represents Christ himself (cf. Luke 10:37: "go and do likewise"). Christ is the one who stays with the weary and faint (Matthew 11:28–30).
19: "When the LORD your God gives you rest from all your enemies in the land he is giving you to possess, you shall blot out the name of Amalek from under heaven. Do not forget." The final blotting-out of Amalek awaits the completion of the rest. The eschatological completion of Christ's victory (Revelation 20:14–15: "death and Hades were thrown into the lake of fire"; names not in the book of life blotted out) is the ultimate fulfillment of the blot-out command. Amalek — the power that preys on the weak and has no fear of God — is the type of every force Christ will finally and completely overthrow. "Do not forget" becomes the eschatological vigilance of the church awaiting his return.
